segments.title: Interwar destroyers preserved harnessed aggression
segments.summary: The lecture argues that interwar Royal Navy destroyers preserved combat edge through aggressive doctrine, independent training, and torpedo-centered force design.
segments.body: The discussion captures the episode's organizing argument. Dr. Clarke links the Harwich Force legacy, objective-led exercises, destroyer officer initiative, and interwar destroyer design from late World War I classes toward the I class and Tribals. The recurring point is that aggression was deliberately harnessed through training, doctrine, torpedo tactics, and ship layouts rather than treated as simple recklessness.

evidence.note: The introduction frames the problem of maintaining fighting edge after war, then describes Harwich Force experience, night-fighting doctrine, and harnessed aggression through objective-led exercises.
The transcript connects destroyer-officer excellence with adaptability and dominance, then moves into late World War I R, S, and V/W class destroyers as the inheritance for interwar development.

The closing development arc says the I class represents the endpoint of the first postwar destroyer generation, ties torpedo layout to aggressive fighting, and explains doctrine as suggestions used to achieve objectives.
